text: The surgeon places an epigastric port and uses suction to gently dissect and separate the omentum, revealing pus flakes. The distended gallbladder with visible gangrenous changes is observed.
The surgeon uses suction to gently sweep away inflammatory adhesions, visibly separating the omentum. Following this, the peri-hepatic and sub-diaphragmatic fluid collections are aspirated.

The surgeon separates the gallbladder from the omentum and colon, then inserts a 5mm working port at the mid-clavicular line and another 5mm port at the anterior axillary line for gallbladder retraction.
The surgeon uses suction to push the gallbladder back, while the assistant employs tooth forceps to assist in repositioning the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses suction and a hook for dissection, while the assistant grasps and retracts the gallbladder. Due to the thickness and edema, the gallbladder is punctured and aspirated to facilitate easier dissection.

The surgeon uses the left hand to compress the duodenum and colon omentum while the right hand operates the suction device to gently manipulate the area. The gallbladder, previously punctured at the site of gangrene, is decompressed with complete aspiration of pus. The assistant then grasps the gallbladder at the mid-body and pushes it back towards the right side to expose the neck.

The surgeon uses gentle suction to create a posterior window and lift the gallbladder from the gallbladder bed. The dissection of Calot's triangle is performed using suction, with sweeping movements to open the area. The cystic artery is visible during this process.
The surgeon uses suction and the left hand working port to access Calot's triangle without the need for an additional assistant port on the left side, allowing compression of the duodenum.

With the neck of the gallbladder freed, the surgeon delineates Calot's triangle, revealing two tubular structures entering the gallbladder. Two-thirds of the gallbladder is lifted from the gallbladder bed, and a clip is applied to the cystic artery.

The surgeon uses gentle suction to keep the surgical field clear, revealing edematous tissue planes. The cystic duct is lifted and defined completely, preparing for clip application. The gallbladder is also lifted, maintaining a clear view for the clip applicator to secure the cystic duct.

The surgeon compresses the bulging omentum using an assistant port on the left side, gaining complete access to the surgical field. The cystic duct is cut, with the upper clip not fully occluding it due to prior gallbladder puncture. Suction is used to lift the gallbladder from the gallbladder bed.

The surgeon dissects the gallbladder from the fundus using a hook, with minor bleeding managed through compression and cautery. The gallbladder is elevated, and the dissection proceeds smoothly without major bleeding.

The surgeon retrieves the gallbladder specimen using a glove bag through the epigastric port. A thorough lavage is performed in all quadrants, particularly cleaning the right upper quadrant with saline. Clips are visible on the cystic artery and duct, with no bleeding from the gallbladder bed. The colon appears grossly dilated due to gallbladder inflammation. A drain is placed in the subhepatic space near the gallbladder fossa through the right-sided port.
